speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: But he's away now and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two more, in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't-- you didn't tell him that we went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't get so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You know, we went back to Grugliasco this past spring.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have all the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went to the place where, where, where my mother-in-law got married. She didn't say that she got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Reel number six, with Judith Rubinstein. You were telling me about your experience at the DP camp in Grugliasco in Italy. So, please, tell me more about that and about--

speaker: interviewee
text: The people, the Italians, were very nice. They're very good. The war was over. They were beaten. They lost many of their people, and they were hungry. But they were really nice to us. The government offered this mental institution for the refugees, where they're coming in from all over. Not just Jews, mainly Jews, but were others, political prisoners, who had to run from the communist regime. And we-- we were just started to-- just for the short while, ended up for almost three years, from '46. The first of April, we crossed the border, and we arrived to Canada in '48, September. So it was quite a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you married? And tell me about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we decided to leave the country. It was no time to get married because it was-- got the order from Budapest, from a Zionist organization, this group now have to leave. And with no papers, we had a couple of make-- this was really not much, but the borders were practically open, was no order yet. The trains didn't run properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived to-- we just packed up whatever we could in a rucksack, in what we could carry on our shoulder, and left everything. Closed the door and I left everything behind. And I had nothing to, to leave, but my husband had. And it was-- everything was, was at no value. We didn't, we didn't count then that-- a silver or a crystal, who needs it? The life we're missing-- and then that-- couldn't replace it. So we arrived to-- From Yugoslavia, we went to Italy, also through the border, arrived to Trieste. We were under house arrest. And it was some manipulation, until we got out from there. Why were you under house arrest there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the three great powers were there, British, French, American. It was an open city, Trieste, at that time, '46. And they saw that there are-- people are coming from the train station without any documents, and they got suspicious. They had an idea of where we wanted to go. They closed the door. We were all housed in a big-- in a community house, formerly belonged to the Jewish community. But the community was not anymore because the Nazis took care of that. And we stayed there for about two weeks. And then, little by little, we-- somehow, we managed to get out from there. And we traveled to Milano, Milan. And from there, we were sent to a DP camp. That was Grugliasco. And we were hoping, just for a short ride, but ended up for a long-- Who sent you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- , [INAUDIBLE]. Organization. They, they took care of people. We were wandering in the street, and they fed us, and we managed. We floated. Alive, that's about it. And some people, the young ones left in these boats. Some arrived, some never made it. Some ended up in Cyprus. And we were just sitting there. In Grugliasco?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grugliasco, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your marriage in Grugliasco.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we arrived in April, and we settled in the beginning of-- the end of May in Grugliasco. We were-- with the traveling, took about a month. And then we decided to get married. And in the garden right there was a rabbi. And we got married. It was not-- no invitation and no fanfare, just got married. Everybody wanted to belong to someone. It was-- life was very miserable. And his-- my husband's family was very nice. They took me in and they became my family. I had no one. And my only brother was still in Czechoslovakia, find his girlfriend, and they got married. And I left message too. They should follow us, but they never-- in the meantime, the Italian border was closed. They could never make it. And they ended up in France. And he took a youth group with him and his wife, young children who were stuck somewhere, they were hiding. I don't know, really, how they made it. And they took them out and [INAUDIBLE] to a boat, legally, to, to, to Israel. And when he arrived, the war started and he went straight into the army. My sister-in-law was a nurse, so they were both fighting in 1948 war. And he was in the '67 war, so went-- and here, I-- at the same time, when they arrived to Israel, we arrived here. So it was communication was-- for a while, it was very shaky.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Grugliasco--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Halifax. And then we had a choice in Montreal to choose one, three places where we wanted to-- Winnipeg, Montreal, and Toronto. We choose Toronto. We didn't know anyone here. And arrived with the five-month-old baby and made home. And I'm very grateful for this country, a wonderful country. Room for everyone--

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that you and your brother parted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he-- first of all, we couldn't-- we couldn't meet. It was-- the borders were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were back in [NON-ENGLISH] together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when, when-- he went to see his girlfriend in Czechoslovakia. By that time, the Russians made difficult to cross the borders. While he was there, my husband's family decided to leave that town. And I left a message for him that as long as-- the minute he returns back to Hungary, should follow us. But he couldn't. By that time, the borders were closed. So we couldn't get together. And I didn't see him for 13 years after that. And I went to visit them in Israel the first time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you had plans to go to Palestine at that time, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that your plans changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the war, because-- and then I had a baby, and it was very dangerous. You know, I-- that one, we were-- I was paranoid, I guess--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the British blockade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the blockade, no. The Arab war started in '48. He was born in March and the war started in May. So we just-- I just couldn't see myself to carry a small baby into the war again. So they were sitting, waiting, hoping that will pass. In the meantime, this [INAUDIBLE] commission from Toronto looking for tradesman. And they picked my husband, his brother, and a cousin of mine who is married to my husband's niece.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 3 families, we came together. But we didn't know anyone in this city. And he learned the trade. He learned the language. We made ourselves a home. And my-- our daughter was born here already, Canada. Where did you live when you first came to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived down in McCall. They wouldn't let us off the train until the Jewish Congress signed that we have a room to stay, because, at that time, it was very difficult to-- with the war was over, was no accommodation, absolutely no. So we got a room somewhere downtown. It's just behind where is the art gallery.

speaker: interviewer
text: On McCall?

speaker: interviewee
text: McCall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Mandel's Dairy? Near Mandel's Dairy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mandel's Creamery? On that block?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: On that block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was two doors away from us. Yes. I wonder, I, I never meet anyone of that people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about it afterwards. I'll tell you how I'm familiar with it. Did you, at any point, become aware or speak to anybody who had actually known the exact fate that befell your mother, or father, or brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had my-- I can talk about myself. I had the odds moments. Surely, I was a rebel, like everybody else. And I just couldn't accept the fact that innocent young people should have such a horrible fate. But then, as I calmed down and I started to think back, and I figured, if I leave and the fate of my father, dead, or everything what was so precious and important to him, if I leave all of this, there's nothing left in my life. And I was clinging to it. My husband felt the same way. So we, we-- right there, it was never a question to decision that we have to follow the-- their footsteps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we always tried to, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: To keep the tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, I think I miscommunicated the question. I wasn't asking about your faith. I was asking about the fate of your parents. Did anybody-- was anybody ever able to tell you what had happened to them? Did you ever meet anybody that was able to tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, they didn't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the one uncle-- there's two uncles who came back. One was in [? Mauthausen, ?] horrible condition. But, somehow, he was an olderly-- he was maybe 45 that time, 50. It seemed like an old man. But, little by little, he went back into same pattern because he didn't feel comfortable being anyone else. That was we were raised in. And it was never a question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war, during your time in Auschwitz and other places, did you think of life after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were thinking only one day at a time. If this day is over, hope we will make it. But it was never a question. I was more religious there than ever before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was clinging to something-- or something had to be that it should let me carry on. I shouldn't go crazy. Because the whole world was crazy around me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were raised in an orthodox family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox. Yes. A modern orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Modern.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very understanding. I mean, I had not so much given that I should be hard to carry, you know, the weight. I enjoyed being-- it was a beautiful life. Everything that my father thought me was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point, did you question your faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, the first year when it was came Yom Kippur and we had the go to say Yiskor, the memorial, you know, prayer for the death. I couldn't. I, I ran out knowing very well that they are not coming back. But I was sort of-- I couldn't accept it. It took me slowly, slowly sunk in. That's how it is. And time goes on. And you know-- and when I started to talk, really, about it, that's what started the healing process. Till then, I just tortured myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why me? And why not the others? Like many others doing the same. But then I started to think that maybe-- Could you-- could you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: --there was purpose of me being here. I should be able to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say why me, can you just explain what do you mean by that? Why me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because others, healthy ones, my friends--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean why did I survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. Why I am the one who, who was, was-- survived and the others didn't? They were healthy and they were young. And then, I don't know. I have no answer for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any-- do you have any thoughts on that question?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I feel now, that definitely since there's such a turmoil in the world, that definitely, I feel that it must have been a purpose of God to let me live, that I should be able to tell. When I see the response of the young people, especially what I am worried about the teenagers, with this, this neo-Nazis coming to defense and trying to sell them this garbage, this theory of super-- superhuman Nazi ideology, it makes me sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I feel that, as long as I can tell them the other side and warn them what, what it could happen, it's because we went through this. It could happen easily, God forbid. Because we have to grab this freedom that we have. And that's only-- I-- I'm not getting anything out of this else, just ears listening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopefully, when they go home, they will think about it, that this old lady was saying, and it's not a fairy tale. It was a real story. And learn from it. I met, a few weeks ago, a German woman who was a bus driver, brought in a group of youngsters from Ajax.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they go first to see the film in the Congress House, and the youngsters, and then they meet a survivor. So I was standing outside. I saw that film so many times. So I ask her, maybe you want to see? She said, oh, no. Oh, no, I know it's a lot of propaganda. How do you know it's six million were killed? My people were killed too. And is telling me with the big tears at how she lost her, her immediate family. So I listen and I listen. And I said, yes, I sympathize with you. I know. I went through your town, Magdeburg, where she came from. I remember how this town looked. It had no roof and it was devastated, and how many innocent people get killed. But whom are you blaming for? It's certainly not our fault. So I left her there and I was telling the kids the story of my life. All of the sudden, the door opens and she walks in, tiptoes in, is sitting in the back and listening. Kids had many questions to ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it was all over, she came over and gave me a big hug and kiss. She said, oh, I'm so glad I came because this part I never heard before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I felt that maybe I just won one and opened their eyes, because surely I felt her pain. She suffered, but not because of us. They were the victims of Hitler's crazy idea in the same way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children loved to listen the story, when I am telling them about that little girl who was won in a beauty contest as the perfect German Aryan child, blue-eyed with curly-- blonde, curly hair. And the war was over, and they find out that this was a Jewish child. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, that just shows you. [INAUDIBLE] kids are from Orientals, and black, and name it, from where-- Canada is a multicultural society. Nobody can claim a pure Aryan-- what does that mean, pure Aryan? I said, now, it just shows you that because she had blue eyes and blonde hair, that made her a better human being? So you judge it to yourself. Do you have dreams about you war experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lately, sweet dreams. I started to talk. I am home many times. Last year, last night, also. But it's not horrible dreams anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your dreams, you are home, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that dream, if you're willing to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I meet my parents and then I see that-- that was very traumatic. For weeks, I couldn't get over. I met and I've walked in, and the house is decorated beautifully. And my-- made a new tiles in the kitchen. And my mother and my father said, you see, I-- to protect your mother from the cold, I made an awning in the front. And I said, that was really considerate of you, Father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was so pleased to see that everything was in order. Big hugs and kisses, and I woke up. Like I was really home. It happens many times. See? The anger went already and I made peace with myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They were young.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a personal level, on a personal level, does the Holocaust have any meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a frightening experience. We-- it's people bound to make mistakes all over again. And you just have to, to respect each other for what we have. That's-- I think this is the whole thing. Politic or brotherhood, I don't know. I always tell the kids, you don't have to love everyone, but respect them. Just because they come from a different culture, a different background, that doesn't mean that they are not as good as you. They were-- they were born to mothers. They carried this child for nine months, and they gave birth with, with the same pain. A blonde child, or the black child, or whatever. It doesn't make any difference. And we have to respect each other for what we are. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has been lost to Jewish communities, to Jewish life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: That pains me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That existed before the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: That pains me, that one and a half million or more little children we lost. If I think about it, that what would happen from that one and a half million. They would be adult now with, with children of their own. My brothers among them. One was brilliant. What we lost, I don't know if we can ever make up for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aside from the material loss, the loss of human beings--

speaker: interviewee
text: Loss of human beings I'm talking about, lives. The material things, I would never cry for that because that was things. You can have-- we came here with a bundle. We didn't have anything. Thank god, we worked hard. We made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, the last-- what I suffered from losing my entire family, old grandfather, grandmother, in their 80s, what a horrible way to die. For why? They'd never hurt a fly. They were not political. Young, innocent children. This is a crime at its--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since the world is a world, the people were always killing each other. But such a systematic murdering, what the Nazis figured out, never ever happened. They're killing now too. But then, nobody raised their voice. Even they did, were very weak voices. Hitler didn't hear it, and he didn't listen to them. He did what-- exactly what he wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why, why was he at that great hatred? I could-- can't figure out. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PHONE RINGING] I know just that we were the victims. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect have your experiences had on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was rebelling for a while, and I was crying a lot. And you know, the smell, and the sound. And even today, yesterday, I went to the dentist. And he was-- she was drilling my teeth. And the smell of that, that burned bone got into my nose. And I got-- I got very restless. It's the slightest thing brings out memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that smell bring back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz, the chimney. The smell, what was lingering over the camp like a, like a tent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Smell of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smell of burned flesh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a horrible smell. It was-- there's a bad country there in this part of Poland, either very cold or very hot. In the morning, we had to leave our place where we slept. It was bitter cold early in the morning, in the warmest days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the evening was cold again. And during the day, we were burning hot. No water, so-- and it was just-- in the air, it was flying, just-- you know, it was-- everything was black. This was flying from the-- out from the chimney, this-- I don't know what. It was soot? What, what was it? Soot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soot. Human flesh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yup. And the clothes, what we got. We never knew that who wore it last. The shoes what I put on belonged to someone before me. My number belonged to someone before me. So it was-- we were just replacements.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean your number belonged to somebody before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what happened-- you can check it out, but I was told-- and then I just-- you know everything was through the grapevine. When someone was killed with this number, they had, they had a file. The Red Cross came from time to time to, to check it out, what everything looks-- and it never looked orderly because we marched out in the same uniform, kerchief. They didn't see that we have no hair. We had a bowl under our arm, marching five in a row, singing. It looked everything fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they looked in the files and they saw a number of people working. But that number didn't exactly belong to that person. That, nobody knew. I mean, the Red Cross certainly didn't know. So then somebody, Mengele, sent walking, and the following day, they gave that number for someone else. That's it. People were not missing, obviously, someone else came who could fill their shoes. So we were a non-person, no name, just a number. How many years have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, '46. How many that makes it? 48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. Two. We have a son and a daughter. And they're good children. I'm very grateful. I didn't think that I would be able to have any children, like none of my friends. Thank God, I didn't go through Mengele's experiment camp, but just being on a-- such a long time on a starvation diet, it's-- a lot of women couldn't have children afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they're good children. I'm very grateful. And we have seven grandchildren. They're all darlings, good, and, and following the parents' footstep. No problem. I am very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this is the real-- what I got for-- for all the pain, what we went through, this is-- I can't find the word for it to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Redemption?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some redemption?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not redemption.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a redemption?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a-- just a, you know, to consolation--

speaker: interviewer
text: Some compensation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Compensation. Yeah, and consolation. Yeah. And I, I hope they will-- we'll try to instill into them the real sense of values, to be good to other people, respect everyone for what they are, and could be-- world could be so beautiful. Today, I saw in the paper, in Ecuador, it's starting a war with their neighbors. Horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to finish up the interview now. So is there anything you'd like to add that you feel you haven't had an opportunity to say? Or is there anything that you would like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that I should mention that this friend of mine, who wrote a book, an Italian lady, I can't remember. Certainly, I can't remember, after 50 years, of her face. But, certainly, she was with me. She wrote a book about her experiences in Auschwitz-Birkenau, and even her number is close to mine. And she was working in the same commando, the same experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, surely, with a different eye because she see-- saw things differently from me. But the- it came down to it, that, really, when I finish reading her book, I was all excited that I know this woman, not remembering her, but I know she was with me. How do I find her? So my son said, call up the Italian consulate. She lives in Genoa. In the back of the book is the information with a picture. And within a half an hour, they called me back with her number.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote to her a letter and you [? saw ?] the beautiful letter she wrote to me, thanking me to come back to her life. And sure, sure, we were together and she remembers this. Of course, she remembers this incidents, what happened to us. While she was-- I was lucky I was liberated by the Americans. She was stopped on the other side of Schwerin, and was ended up by the Russians. That's another story. And little by little, got back to Italy. She didn't find anyone and nothing. And she started right away to-- she was a teacher. She's a newspaper woman, and well-known. She was a friend of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liana Millu, M-I-L-L-U. So I started to correspond with her. And I got a very warm response. And then I invited her to come and visit us. And she wrote that she's too old to travel. Maybe one day we will meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my son, who was longing to go back to Italy to see the birthplace where he was born, decided that we will go. And Renee, my daughter-in-law came along. And the three of us did a trip last May. And it was an experience that I will never forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the camp. We find the room. The, the garden I showed here--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is at Grugliasco, that you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grugliasco. And then a stranger took us into Torino. They find a hospital where he was born. And we met Liana in Genoa. It was a very beautiful reunion. And we are in touch. We're corresponding. I hope maybe one day she will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Rubinstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.
